items.question_text: When should ATC light signals be used to control aircraft or movement-area vehicles, equipment, and personnel?

choices.text: When radio communications cannot be employed.
Whenever visual control would be faster than using the radio.
Only during nighttime operations.
Only for aircraft already cleared for takeoff or landing.

items.explanation: ATC light signals are a control method for situations where radio communications cannot be used.

items.question_text: Use ATC light signals from Table 3-2-1 to control aircraft and the movement of vehicles, equipment, and personnel on the movement area when radio communications cannot be employed.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the controlling instruction in 3-2-1.

items.question_text: The light-signal procedure applies to aircraft and to vehicles, equipment, and personnel on the movement area when radio communications cannot be employed.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph's scope covers both aircraft and movement-area traffic when radios cannot be used.

items.question_text: In Table 3-2-1, what does a steady green signal mean for an aircraft on the ground?

choices.text: Cleared for takeoff.
Cleared to taxi.
Return to starting point on the airport.
Airport unsafe. Do not land.

items.explanation: Table 3-2-1 shows that steady green means cleared for takeoff to an aircraft on the ground.

items.situation: A vehicle operating on the movement area loses radio contact, but you still need to control its movement. Another controller says the vehicle can continue because light signals are mainly for aircraft, not ground traffic.
items.para_context: ATC light signals from Table 3-2-1 are used to control aircraft and the movement of vehicles, equipment, and personnel on the movement area when radio communications cannot be employed.

choices.text: Use the appropriate ATC light signal to control the vehicle's movement on the movement area.
Allow the vehicle to continue without control because light signals are only for aircraft in flight.
Treat the vehicle as uncontrolled until radio communications are restored because the table does not apply to ground traffic.
Issue instructions by airport lighting changes because the table may be used only when both radio and visual signals fail.

items.explanation: The paragraph expressly includes movement-area vehicles, equipment, and personnel within the light-signal procedure.

items.question_text: What does the alternating red-and-green warning signal mean?

choices.text: General warning; exercise extreme caution.
Stop immediately and remain stopped until a steady green signal is received.
Return to the starting point on the airport.
Cleared to cross or proceed normally.

items.explanation: Alternating red and green is the general warning signal meaning exercise extreme caution.

items.question_text: Direct a general warning signal, alternating red and green, to aircraft or vehicle operators when a collision hazard exists, mechanical trouble may be unknown to the pilot, or other hazardous conditions require intensified alertness.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those are the listed conditions for directing the warning signal.

items.instruction: Which condition calls for the general warning signal?
items.question_text: Which condition calls for directing the alternating red-and-green general warning signal?

choices.text: Mechanical trouble exists of which the pilot might not be aware.
The pilot is established on final and already receiving normal runway clearances.
The aircraft has acknowledged all previous light signals correctly.
The airport is closed only for routine mowing away from all movement areas.

items.explanation: Possible mechanical trouble unknown to the pilot is one of the listed triggers for the warning signal.

items.instruction: Is this warning-signal scope statement correct?
items.question_text: The general warning signal applies as a caution alert, not a prohibitive signal, and may be followed by another light signal as circumstances permit.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note says the warning signal is not itself prohibitive and can be followed by another appropriate light signal.

items.situation: Two aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists. Another controller says to wait until one aircraft actually turns toward the other before using the general warning signal because alternating red and green is reserved for mechanical failures.
items.para_context: The alternating red-and-green general warning signal is used when aircraft are converging and a collision hazard exists, when possible mechanical trouble may be unknown to the pilot, or when other hazardous conditions require intensified alertness.

choices.text: Direct the general warning signal now because a collision hazard is one of the listed uses.
Wait until an impact is imminent because the warning signal may not be used for traffic conflicts.
Use steady red instead because alternating red and green is only for airport-surface hazards.
Do nothing unless the pilot first reports uncertainty about the other aircraft.

items.explanation: A converging-aircraft collision hazard is the first listed condition for the general warning signal.

items.question_text: A fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only is airborne during daylight hours. How should it acknowledge a light signal?

choices.text: Rock the wings.
Flash navigation or landing lights.
Move ailerons or rudder while in flight.
Turn toward the tower and flash the landing light.

items.explanation: A daylight fixed-wing acknowledgment in flight is wing rocking.

items.question_text: A fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only must flash navigation or landing lights between sunset and sunrise to acknowledge a light signal.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Nighttime fixed-wing acknowledgment is by flashing navigation or landing lights.

items.instruction: Is this fixed-wing acknowledgment condition correct?
items.question_text: If a fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only is on the ground between sunrise and sunset, the pilot must move ailerons or rudder to acknowledge the light signal.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Ground acknowledgment by a daylight fixed-wing aircraft is movement of ailerons or rudder.

items.instruction: Which is an approved helicopter acknowledgment?
items.question_text: Which daylight acknowledgment is approved for a hovering helicopter equipped with receiver only?

choices.text: Turn the helicopter toward the controlling facility and flash the landing light, or rock the tip path plane.
Move ailerons or rudder while hovering.
Rock the wings while hovering.
Flash navigation lights only, because landing lights are reserved for nighttime acknowledgment.

items.explanation: A hovering helicopter in daylight may acknowledge by turning toward the facility and flashing the landing light or by rocking the tip path plane.

items.question_text: In Table 3-2-1, what does a flashing white signal mean for movement of vehicles, equipment, and personnel?

choices.text: Return to starting point on the airport.
Cleared to cross or proceed.
Stop.
Clear the taxiway or runway.

items.explanation: Table 3-2-1 assigns flashing white the meaning return to starting point on the airport for movement-area ground traffic.

items.situation: A receiver-only helicopter is hovering during daylight and needs to acknowledge your light signal. Another controller says the pilot should rock the wings because that is the normal daylight acknowledgment for aircraft in flight.
items.para_context: Receiver-only acknowledgments depend on aircraft type, whether the aircraft is on the ground or in flight, and whether it is day or night. A hovering helicopter in daylight acknowledges by turning toward the facility and flashing the landing light or by rocking the tip path plane.

choices.text: Request the helicopter to turn toward the facility and flash the landing light, or rock the tip path plane.
Request the helicopter to rock the wings because all aircraft in flight use the same daytime acknowledgment.
Request movement of ailerons or rudder because hovering helicopters use the fixed-wing ground acknowledgment.
Request flashing navigation lights only because landing lights may be used only after sunset.

items.explanation: Helicopter acknowledgments differ from fixed-wing acknowledgments and depend on the helicopter's flight state and time of day.

items.question_text: When should ATC light signals be used to control aircraft or movement-area vehicles, equipment, and personnel?
items.explanation: ATC light signals are used when radio communications cannot be employed.

choices.text: When radio communications cannot be employed.
Whenever visual control would simply be faster than the radio.
Only during hours of darkness.
Only after an aircraft has been cleared for takeoff or landing.

items.question_text: ATC light signals may be used to control both aircraft and movement-area vehicles, equipment, and personnel when radio communications cannot be employed.
items.explanation: The paragraph's scope includes aircraft and movement-area ground traffic.

items.question_text: What document does 3-2-1 direct controllers to use for the meanings of ATC light signals?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifically points to Table 3-2-1 for the signal meanings.

choices.text: Table 3-2-1.
The facility directive for runway scanning.
Table 3-9-1.
The low approach phraseology section.

items.question_text: Under Table 3-2-1, what does flashing green mean to an aircraft on the ground?
items.explanation: Table 3-2-1 shows that flashing green means cleared to taxi for an aircraft on the ground.

choices.text: Cleared to taxi.
Cleared for takeoff.
Stop.
Return to starting point on the airport.

items.question_text: A vehicle on the movement area loses radio communications but still needs ATC control. What does 3-2-1 authorize?
items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly authorizes use of ATC light signals to control movement-area vehicles, equipment, and personnel when radios cannot be used.

choices.text: Use ATC light signals to control the vehicle's movement.
Treat the vehicle as uncontrolled until the radio is restored.
Use ATC light signals only for aircraft, not for vehicles.
Continue control only by airport vehicle escort rather than by ATC signal.

items.question_text: What does the alternating red-and-green warning signal mean?
items.explanation: Alternating red and green is the general warning signal meaning exercise extreme caution.

choices.text: General warning; exercise extreme caution.
Stop immediately and do not move.
Return to the starting point on the airport.
Cleared to cross or proceed.

items.question_text: The general warning signal is prohibitive and may not be followed by any other light signal.
items.explanation: The note says the warning signal is not prohibitive and can be followed by another light signal as circumstances permit.

items.question_text: Which of the following is one of the listed conditions for directing the general warning signal?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists collision hazard, mechanical trouble unknown to the pilot, and other hazardous conditions requiring heightened alertness.

choices.text: Mechanical trouble exists of which the pilot might not be aware.
The pilot has already acknowledged every prior light signal correctly.
The aircraft is following the normal taxi route without conflict.
The airport is closed for routine field maintenance away from all movement areas.

items.question_text: A collision hazard exists between converging aircraft. What light-signal response is listed for that situation?
items.explanation: A collision hazard between converging aircraft is one of the listed uses of the alternating red-and-green warning signal.

choices.text: The alternating red-and-green general warning signal.
A flashing white signal ordering return to the starting point.
A steady green signal because traffic is already moving.
No light signal unless the pilot first reports the other aircraft in sight.

items.question_text: Which note-based statement about the general warning signal is correct?
items.explanation: The note says the warning signal is not prohibitive and may be followed by another light signal as circumstances permit.

choices.text: It is not prohibitive and may be followed by another light signal as circumstances permit.
It cancels all other light-signal options until radio communications are restored.
It is used only for aircraft and not for vehicle operators.
It can be used only for mechanical failures, not for traffic or airport-surface hazards.

items.question_text: A fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only is airborne during daylight hours. How should it acknowledge a light signal?
items.explanation: A daylight fixed-wing acknowledgment in flight is wing rocking.

choices.text: Rock the wings.
Flash navigation or landing lights.
Move ailerons or rudder while in flight.
Turn toward the tower and flash the landing light.

items.question_text: A fixed-wing aircraft equipped with receiver only must flash navigation or landing lights between sunset and sunrise to acknowledge a light signal.
items.explanation: That is the nighttime fixed-wing acknowledgment method.

items.question_text: Which of the following is one approved daylight acknowledgment for a hovering helicopter equipped with receiver only?
items.explanation: A hovering helicopter in daylight may acknowledge by turning toward the controlling facility and flashing the landing light or by rocking the tip path plane.

choices.text: Turn toward the controlling facility and flash the landing light, or rock the tip path plane.
Move ailerons or rudder while hovering.
Rock the wings while hovering.
Flash navigation lights only because landing lights may be used only after sunset.

items.question_text: Under Table 3-2-1, what does steady red mean to an aircraft in flight?
items.explanation: Table 3-2-1 assigns steady red the meaning give way to other aircraft and continue circling for an aircraft in flight.

choices.text: Give way to other aircraft and continue circling.
Airport unsafe. Do not land.
Return for landing, to be followed by steady green at the proper time.
Cleared to land.

items.question_text: A receiver-only helicopter is hovering during daylight and needs to acknowledge your light signal. Which response is correct?
items.explanation: Helicopter acknowledgments differ from fixed-wing acknowledgments and depend on whether the helicopter is hovering or in flight.

choices.text: Turn toward the controlling facility and flash the landing light, or rock the tip path plane.
Rock the wings because all aircraft in daylight use the same in-flight acknowledgment.
Move ailerons or rudder because hovering helicopters use the fixed-wing ground acknowledgment.
Flash navigation lights because helicopters may not use landing lights for acknowledgment in daylight.

items.question_text: Under Table 3-2-1, what does flashing white mean to movement of vehicles, equipment, and personnel?
items.explanation: For movement-area vehicles, equipment, and personnel, flashing white means return to starting point on the airport.

choices.text: Return to starting point on the airport.
Cleared to cross or proceed.
Stop.
Clear the taxiway or runway.
